highlights.item: Payload is pointed at Supabase Postgres with migrations intentionally gated behind explicit user approval.
Bootstrap and seed flows exist for the first admin and repeatable content setup.
Payload MCP is now part of the repo process as an env-gated plugin path, with editor setup documented for Cursor and Codex and local configs normalized to `localhost:5465`.
A pending migration file now exists for Payload MCP API keys and the remaining quote schema changes, but it has not been applied.
The customer login flow is now customer-only, while admin access stays on Payload admin and the internal ops dashboard lives on a separate `/ops` route.

The portal sidebar and route boundaries now keep customers on account-centric pages (`/dashboard`, `/estimates`, `/invoices`, `/service-schedule`, `/account`) instead of exposing docs/admin-oriented navigation.

Admin impersonation is now first-party: a real-admin/effective-user auth context, admin-only impersonation routes, and a floating preview toolbar persist across frontend and portal pages so staff can preview customer scope without losing admin identity.

A vendored image-generation MCP server is now part of the repo workflow under `vendor/image-gen-mcp`, with a self-contained `uv` environment, root `.env` passthrough, Cursor/Codex config examples, and an env-gated `npm run dev` hook so image tooling can run alongside Next when needed.

next.item: Approve and run the database migration window before enabling any new schema-backed tooling such as Payload MCP API keys.
Create the first durable admin account in the real database.
After migration approval, enable Payload MCP, restart dev, and generate an API key for local editor use.
Decide whether admin sign-in should remain at `/admin/login` or later get a branded dedicated team-login route.
Backfill any real production customer accounts to the new portal profile fields if existing contacts need billing/service addresses immediately.
Use the new image MCP workflow in phase 03 to generate and curate hero, feature, and background assets, then decide which ones belong in Payload media versus `public/`.
